coredata.dc:title: A nanozyme multifunctional platform based on iron doped carbon dots derived from Tibetan Ganoderma lucidum waste for glucose sensing, anti-counterfeiting applications, and anticancer cell effect
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linjie
Zheng, Shujun
Liu, Yan
Ji, Yang
Liu, Xiaoya
Wang, Fei
Li, Caolong
coredata.dc:description: Implementing the concept of turning waste into treasure, the conversion of biomass waste into high-value carbon materials, especially carbon dots (CDs), has pointed out a new direction for disease diagnosis, tumor treatment, and other aspects. In this work, we have reported the GL-CDs(Fe) via a simple synthesis route exploiting Ganoderma lucidum waste as the precursor. Thanks to their excellent optical property and peroxidase mimetic activity, a novel GL-CDs(Fe)-based ratio fluorescence/colorimetric/smartphone triple mode sensing platform is cleverly fabricated for glucose determination with the LOD of 0.28, 0.37, and 0.52 μΜ separately. Especially, this triple mode biosensor is successfully utilized for glucose detection in serum samples with the relative error of less than ±8 % compared with clinical reports. Surprisingly, the GL-CDs(Fe) also presents immense application prospects in high-level anti-counterfeiting aspects due to their excellent luminescent properties, high water-solubility, and easy availability. Furthermore, GL-CDs(Fe) can catalyze excessive H2O2 inside tumor cells to produce massive hydroxyl radicals (·OH) which break down the redox levels of cancer cells and thereby eliminate tumor cells. Thus, this integrated “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform based on GL-CDs(Fe) unveils enormous research and application prospects for bio-sensing, anti-counterfeiting, cancer treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ganoderma lucidum waste
Triple mode sensing platform
High-level anti-counterfeiting
Eliminate tumor cells
“Three-in-One” multifunctional platform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescent properties of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
Fluorescence and colorimetric determination of H2O2
Determination of glucose via triple modes
Preparation of anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent patterns
Synthesis and characterization of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
Optical characteristics and peroxidase mimetic activities of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
Ratio fluorescence and colorimetric determination of H2O2
Ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric dual mode sensor for glucose determination
Analysis of glucose via smartphone-based visual sensing platform
Application of anti-counterfeiting ink and anticancer cell effect in vitro based on GL-CDs(Fe)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301YD0018C) and Key Research and Development Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301ZY0015G). The China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M733894), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20231021) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2632023GR26). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, and so on.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation procedures (A) and XRD profiles (B) of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe). (C) The TEM image of GL-CDs(Fe) and corresponding particle size distribution. (D) Zeta potentials of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe). (E) The FT-IR spectra of GL, GL-CDs, and GL-CDs(Fe). The high-resolution C 1s (F), N 1s (G), O 1s (H), and Fe 2p (I) XPS spectra of GL-CDs(Fe).

The UV–vis and fluorescence patterns of GL-CDs(Fe) (A) (Inset is the picture under sunlight and UV lamp). (B) The emission spectra under diverse excitation lights. (C) The UV–vis profiles of diverse reaction systems. (D) Comparison of fluorescence intensity and peroxidase mimetic activity between GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe). (E) The UV–vis patterns of GL-CDs(Fe) + various chromogenic substrates (Inset is corresponding real photo). (F) The EPR profile of GL-CDs(Fe) + H2O2 + DMPO system and related catalytic mechanism.

(A) The diagram of H2O2 sensing through proposed dual mode sensor. The feasibility of fluorescence (B) and colorimetric (C) determination of H2O2. (D) The fluorescence spectra of GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system with different contents of H2O2, and (E) the related calibration plot between FL/S and ln[H2O2]. (F) The UV–vis patterns of GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system with different concentrations of H2O2, and (G) the corresponding calibration plot between A652 and ln[H2O2].

(A) Schematic diagram of glucose detection by proposed dual-mode sensor. (B) Fluorescence spectra of GOx + GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system with diverse contents of glucose, and (C) the related calibration plot between FL/S and ln[GLU]. (D) The UV–vis patterns of GOx + GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system with different levels of glucose, and (E) the corresponding calibration plot between A652 and ln[GLU]. The selective detection of glucose via fluorescence (F) and colorimetric (G) modes.
(A) The illustration of glucose analysis via smartphone-assisted visual sensor platform, and (B) corresponding linear fitting plot.

(A) The photographic images of fluorescent pattern under ambient light and a 365 nm UV light using the aqueous solution of GL-CDs(Fe). (B) The live−dead assay of HepG2 cells stained by Calcein-AM/PI with or without GL-CDs(Fe) treatment.
The construction and application of “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform.

head.ce:title: A nanozyme multifunctional platform based on iron doped carbon dots derived from Tibetan Ganoderma lucidum waste for glucose sensing, anti-counterfeiting applications, and anticancer cell effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Implementing the concept of turning waste into treasure, the conversion of biomass waste into high-value carbon materials, especially carbon dots (CDs), has pointed out a new direction for disease diagnosis, tumor treatment, and other aspects. In this work, we have reported the GL-CDs(Fe) via a simple synthesis route exploiting Ganoderma lucidum waste as the precursor. Thanks to their excellent optical property and peroxidase mimetic activity, a novel GL-CDs(Fe)-based ratio fluorescence/colorimetric/smartphone triple mode sensing platform is cleverly fabricated for glucose determination with the LOD of 0.28, 0.37, and 0.52 μΜ separately. Especially, this triple mode biosensor is successfully utilized for glucose detection in serum samples with the relative error of less than ±8 % compared with clinical reports. Surprisingly, the GL-CDs(Fe) also presents immense application prospects in high-level anti-counterfeiting aspects due to their excellent luminescent properties, high water-solubility, and easy availability. Furthermore, GL-CDs(Fe) can catalyze excessive H2O2 inside tumor cells to produce massive hydroxyl radicals (·OH) which break down the redox levels of cancer cells and thereby eliminate tumor cells. Thus, this integrated “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform based on GL-CDs(Fe) unveils enormous research and application prospects for bio-sensing, anti-counterfeiting, cancer treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the concept of turning waste into treasure, green and low-carbon has gradually become a new trend due to the increasingly severe energy crisis and environmental pollution [1,2]. Biomass waste, as a substitute for some non-renewable raw materials, can achieve value-added transformation, save resources, and protect environment [3,4]. Especially, the conversion of biomass wastes into valuable carbon materials has aroused widespread interest among researchers [5,6]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a zero-dimensional carbon-based optical nanomaterial, have been extensively applied to fluorescent sensing, biological imaging, and optical security because of their unique optical properties, ecological friendliness, superior biocompatibility, and ease of surface modification [7,8]. For instance, Zhang et al. synthesized multi-color CDs from waste leather for anti-counterfeiting applications [9]. Ren et al. prepared low toxicity CDs obtained from peach leaf waste for in vitro/in vivo biological imaging [10]. Likewise, coffee grounds were served as raw material to design a satisfactory fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid detection [11]. Hence, multifunctional CDs synthesized through the green pathway of biomass waste play a tremendous function in multiple fields.

Interestingly, the doping of certain active metal elements such as Fe, Ni, Cu, and Mn in CDs can not only regulate their luminescent properties, but also endow them with excellent enzyme-mimetic activity [12]. This metal doped CDs is a type of nanozyme that is mainly utilized in the following two aspects: (1) the fabrication of colorimetric or fluorescent sensors for analysis of diverse targets based on the reaction between its catalytic intermediate and chromogenic substrate; (2) They can regulate intracellular redox levels via reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by catalytic substrates, which can affect cell activity and eliminate cancer cells [13]. Thereby, many researchers are gradually developing various metal doped CDs for disease diagnosis and treatment, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory [14,15]. For example, Yao et al. prepared CDs from coffee for cancer treatment via inducing ferroptosis in cancer cells [16]. Moreover, green CDs derived from broccoli extract was also treated as the antioxidant nanopharmaceutics for inflammatory therapy [17]. Regrettably, the use of these CDs is relatively single, so developing multifunctional CDs to broaden their application range is of great potential value.

In this research, the GL-CDs(Fe) with excellent optical property and peroxidase like activity have been synthetized by using Ganoderma lucidum waste as the raw material, which serves as the core component of “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform for glucose sensing, anti-counterfeiting ink, and anticancer cells (Scheme 1 ). Specifically, because of the coordination between peroxidase mimetic activity of GL-CDs(Fe) and intense luminescence of TMB, as well as fluorescence game between the two, a novel ratio fluorescence and colorimetric biosensor is reasonably proposed for glucose analysis. Compared to other mode sensors, these fluorescent or colorimetric modes possess some unique advantages such as sensitive response, simple test process, low cost, and visual read-out [18]. Surprisingly, a smartphone-based visual sensing platform has been designed to rapidly and conveniently determine glucose. Compared with single detection mode, this three-mode sensing platform can self-verify the analysis results and improve the accuracy of detection via self-correction function [19–22]. Meanwhile, the GL-CDs(Fe) solution is uniformly dispersed for the preparation of a simple anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink due to their excellent luminescent properties, high water-solubility, and easy availability, which are expected to be applied in high-level anti-counterfeiting aspects. More significantly, due to their excellent peroxidase-mimetic activity, GL-CDs(Fe) can catalyze excessive H2O2 to produce massive hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which break down the redox levels of cancer cells and thereby eliminate tumor cells. Accordingly, this “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform based on GL-CDs(Fe) has enormous application prospects in multiple biochemical fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Preparation of GL-CDs(Fe) and GL-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dregs of Ganoderma lucidum decoction were completely dried at 50 °C, and then smashed into powder. For the synthesis of GL-CDs(Fe), 0.5 g of Ganoderma lucidum powder and 0.3 g of FeCl3·6H2O were added into 20 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated until sufficiently dissolved. Subsequently, the reaction was conducted in a Teflon autoclave at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the yellow-brown solution was obtained by centrifugation and filtration. Then, the filtered solution was further treated with dialysis (Mw = 1000 Da) for 12 h to remove excessive precursors and by-products. Finally, the ash black powder was then collected via freeze-drying treatment for further experiments and characterization. For comparison, the GL-CDs were prepared using only Ganoderma lucidum powder by same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe) were studied in this experiment. Briefly, a fluorimeter cuvette was added with 950 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) and then blended with 50 μL GL-CDs or GL-CDs(Fe) (1.5 mg mL−1). Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by spectrophotometer at diverse excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase like activity of GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-mimic performance of GL-CDs(Fe) was assessed through catalytic oxidation of chromogenic substrates (TMB, OPD or ABTS) in the presence of H2O2. In brief, 50 μL GL-CDs(Fe) (1.5 mg mL−1), 50 μL H2O2 (1 mM), and 70 μL TMB (5 mM) were added into 830 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) and co-incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. Next, the absorbance value at 652 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric determination of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection of H2O2 was conducted as follows: 50 μL H2O2 (various levels) were injected into 830 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) containing 50 μL GL-CDs(Fe) (1.5 mg mL−1) and 70 μL TMB (5 mM). Above series of mixed solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 15 min, and the fluorescence spectra of these mixed solution were obtained by FL spectrophotometer under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The colorimetric assay for H2O2 detection was performed in the same way, and the absorbance signals at 652 nm of reaction solutions were monitored by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose via triple modes
ce:section.ce:para: For glucose determination, 50 μL GOx (0.5 mg mL−1) and 50 μL glucose (diverse contents) were added into 100 μL Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.4), and the mingled solution was reacted at 37 °C for 25 min. Then, the foregoing solution was blended with 680 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), 50 μL GL-CDs(Fe) (1.5 mg mL−1), and 70 μL TMB (5 mM) in sequence, and further co-incubated for another 15 min. Afterwards, this solution was added to quartz cuvette for fluorescence and UV–vis spectral measurements. Additionally, 300 μL aforesaid reaction solution was injected into the cover of centrifuge tube and the corresponding photograph was captured by smartphone equipped with color recognition software (Color Grab), which could convert the color information of photograph into RGB values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The GL-CDs(Fe) powder (5 mg) was dissolved in 1 mL ultrapure water through ultrasound to prepare fluorescent ink, which was applied to paint various patterns on the filter paper by using cotton swab or fine writing brush.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer cell effect of GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The live/dead cell staining measurement was performed by utilizing Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) to further evaluate the anticancer cell effect of GL-CDs(Fe). Briefly, HepG2 cell was cultivated in DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotics at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h, and then were divided into three groups as follow: The first group was incubated without GL-CDs(Fe) or H2O2 for 4 h; the second group was incubated with only GL-CDs(Fe) (250 μg mL−1) for 4 h; the last group was incubated with GL-CDs(Fe) (250 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (0.5 mM) for 4 h. Subsequently, all groups were stained by Calcein AM/PI reagent for 20 min, and then the cells were washed with PBS prior to imaging by fluorescent inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: Using Ganoderma lucidum waste as the raw material, the GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe) were synthetized via classic hydrothermal approach (Fig. 1 A). Subsequently, the microscopic morphology and surface functional groups of obtained CDs were characterized through several means. As depicted in Fig. 1B–a broad diffraction signal at 20.5° is attributed to (002) plane of sp2 graphitic carbon, but the intensity of this characteristic peak in GL-CDs(Fe) is much lower than that of GL-CDs, suggesting the successful doping of iron [23]. Evidently, the GL-CDs(Fe) unveils uniformly distributed quasi-spherical shape with an average size of approximately 4.09 nm (Fig. 1C). Moreover, the Zeta potentials of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe) were determined as −15.8 mV and −23.6 mV separately (Fig. 1D), indicating that the doping of Fe endows CDs with more electronegativity. Additionally, the surface groups and element valence of two CDs were surveyed by FT-IR and XPS means. Apparently, the FT-IR characteristic peak of GL-CDs is basically consistent with that of Ganoderma lucidum waste. Specifically, the characteristic signals at 2935 and 1436 cm−1 correspond to C–H and C–N, respectively [24]. Furthermore, an emphatic broad peak at about 3415 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibration of O–H, and absorption signals at 1654 and 1060 cm−1 belong to CO and C–OH separately. These plentiful oxygenated groups testify satisfactory water-solubility of prepared CDs [25,26]. Interestingly, two emerging peaks located at 442 and 520 cm−1 in the GL-CDs(Fe) are related to the Fe–O bond, testifying the successful doping of Fe into CDs (Fig. 1E) [23]. Compared with GL-CDs (Fig. S1A), the phenomenon of additional Fe element appearing in the survey XPS spectrum of GL-CDs(Fe) is also cross validated with FT-IR result (Fig. S2). Moreover, the C 1s spectrum of GL-CDs presents three peaks corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CO respectively (Fig. S1B), and three clear signals belonging to C–N–C (398.9 eV), pyrrole nitrogen (399.5 eV), and N-(C)3 (400.8 eV) are observed (Fig. S1C), which are similar to high-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra of GL-CDs(Fe) (Fig. 1F and G). In the O 1s profile of GL-CDs (Fig. S1D), two characteristic peaks at 531.2 and 532.3 eV are attributed to C–O and CO bonds separately [27]. However, the O 1s pattern of GL-CDs(Fe) reveals two apparent signals corresponding to C–O/Fe–O and CO groups (Fig. 1H) [24], further confirming the successful preparation of GL-CDs(Fe). Especially, the Fe 2p profile of GL-CDs(Fe) (Fig. 1I) unveils two pairs of peaks situated in 711.2 eV (Fe2+ 2p3/2), 715.6 eV (Fe3+ 2p3/2), 724.2 eV (Fe2+ 2p1/2) and 727.8 eV (Fe3+ 2p1/2) [23]. This attests the occurrence of plentiful Fe2+/Fe3+ redox couples, which endow GL-CDs(Fe) with excellent enzyme mimetic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics and peroxidase mimetic activities of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics of GL-CDs and GL-CDs(Fe) were investigated via UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopies. As presented in Fig. 2 A–a typical absorption signal of GL-CDs(Fe) at 216 nm originates from π→π* transition of CC bond, and the shoulder peak located at 282 nm corresponds to n→π* transition of CO group [27,28]. Obviously, the maximum emission intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) is recorded at 440 nm under the excitation of 350 nm, presenting blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 2A). The emission peak position and intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) undergo significant variations as the excitation signal changes from 320 to 380 nm (Fig. 2B), suggesting its excitation-dependent optical feature. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of GL-CDs(Fe) is calculated as 6.26 % by selecting quinine sulfate as a reference. Notably, the GL-CDs presents almost consistent absorption and fluorescence trends to GL-CDs(Fe) (Fig. S3), while the fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) is much weaker than that of GL-CDs owing to the Fe doping (Fig. 2D). Moreover, the effects of various factors including buffer pH, ionic strength, irradiation time, and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) were explored to estimate the luminescent constancy of GL-CDs(Fe). With the adjustment of pH from 3 to 8, the emission intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) basically maintains constant (Fig. S4A), suggesting that GL-CDs(Fe) possesses ideal luminescence stability over a wide pH range. Similarly, the variations of NaCl level only cause slight fluctuations in FL intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) (Fig. S4B), exhibiting its excellent resistance to high ion strength circumstance. More importantly, no significant alteration occurs in the fluorescence signal of GL-CDs(Fe) under continuous irradiation of UV lamp (Fig. S4C), displaying the excellent anti-photobleaching ability of GL-CDs(Fe). Additionally, GL-CDs(Fe) still remains relatively stable fluorescence value within 30 days (Fig. S4D). These foregoing test outcomes illustrate good optical stability of GL-CDs(Fe), which can provide strong support for subsequent related experiments.

Meaningly, the doping of Fe endows GL-CDs(Fe) with distinctive peroxidase-like performance. The related catalytic reactions were conducted to explore the peroxidase-mimetic activity of GL-CDs(Fe). Only when GL-CDs(Fe) is co-incubated with TMB and H2O2, a prominent absorption peak occurs at 652 nm, accompanied by a variation from colorless to blue in solution system (Fig. 2C). This implies that GL-CDs(Fe) can catalyze H2O2 to form oxidative intermediates, which further oxidize TMB into TMBox. Unfortunately, GL-CDs fail to possess such enzymatic catalytic ability and only exhibit superior fluorescence intensity compared to GL-CDs(Fe) (Fig. 2D), which proves that doped Fe plays a decisive part in the catalytic behavior of GL-CDs(Fe). To present the optimum catalytic ability of GL-CDs(Fe), several reaction parameters including buffer pH and incubation time were optimized. Evidently, GL-CDs(Fe) displays high pH dependence and the best catalytic capacity appears at pH 4 (Fig. S5). Evidently, the peak value at 652 nm significantly raises as the reaction time prolongs from 1 to 15 min, but it increases slowly after 15 min (Fig. S6). To balance detection performance and reaction time, the incubation time is set to 15 min. Hence, the subsequent tests were conducted under optimum conditions (pH = 4 buffer and incubation time for 15 min).

Similarly, other chromogenic substrates such as OPD or ABTS have been selected to co-incubate with GL-CDs(Fe), the colorless substrate-H2O2 systems correspondingly transform into yellow or green with characteristic signals at 450 nm (OPDOX) or 417 nm (ABTSOX) separately (Fig. 2E). To uncover the reaction process of GL-CDs(Fe), EPR measurement was performed to monitor the formation of possible intermediates. As depicted in Fig. 2F–a distinct characteristic EPR spectrum belonging to hydroxyl radical (·OH) is obtained when GL-CDs(Fe), H2O2, and capturing agent (DMPO) are blended [29]. Therefore, the possible catalytic mechanism of GL-CDs(Fe) was assumed as follows: the GL-CDs(Fe) with plentiful Fe2+/Fe3+ redox couples can activate Fenton reaction to generate abundant ·OH in the existence of exogenous H2O2, which then triggers the conversion of colorless substrates (TMB, OPD, and ABTS) into the corresponding colored oxidation products (TMBox, OPDox, and ABTSox). To quantitatively assess the peroxidase-mimicking catalytic capacity of GL-CDs(Fe), related steady-state kinetic assays were conducted by utilizing TMB and H2O2 as substrates [30]. As expected, the correlation between initial velocity (V) and substrate concentration (S) of two substrates accords with the Michaelis-Mending model (Fig. S7). Correspondingly, the Michaelis constant (Km) and maximum initial velocity (Vmax) of TMB and H2O2 have been calculated and listed in Table S1. Visibly, the GL-CDs(Fe) unveils a lower Km compared to horseradish peroxidase (HRP), suggesting their stronger substrate affinity toward TMB and H2O2. Meanwhile, the Vmax of GL-CDs(Fe) is comparable or even better than that of other reported nanozymes. More significantly, the GL-CDs(Fe) exhibits a satisfactory robustness against harsh incubation pH and temperature (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio fluorescence and colorimetric determination of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Ingeniously, the fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system significantly increases and peak position undergoes the blue-shift simultaneously, as the pH of this system changes from alkaline to acidic. Nevertheless, the fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) alone in pH 4 buffer is extremely weak compared to GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system (Fig. S9). This phenomenon illustrates that individual TMB fluorescence plays a leading role for GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system in acidic environment, which is consistent with the outcomes in Fig. S10. Due to the difference in peak positions between GL-CDs(Fe) and TMB, the game between the two can cause the variations in peak position and fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system. Specifically, GL-CDs(Fe) catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce TMBox in the presence of H2O2, which leads to the weaking of fluorescence intensity and red-shift of peak position in GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system due to the consumption of TMB (Fig. 3 B). Meanwhile, the enhancement of UV–vis absorption signal at 652 nm is attributed to the generation of TMBox, providing an approach of colorimetric sensing H2O2 (Fig. 3C). In view of the coordination between peroxidase mimetic activity of GL-CDs(Fe) and intense luminescence of TMB, as well as the fluorescence game between the two, the strategy of new ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode detection of H2O2 is reasonably proposed (Fig. 3A). To realize excellent detection performance, the optimization of TMB dosage and reaction time were carried out. As proved by Fig. S11, the fluorescence signal value of this reaction system distinctly increases and overflows the range with the raising of TMB concentration. Thus, the reasonable dosage of TMB is set as 0.35 mM. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of GL-CDs(Fe) + H2O2+TMB system first decreases and then reaches dynamic equilibrium as the incubation time prolongs (Fig. S12). To balance detection performance and testing speed, 15 min is chosen as optimal reaction time in the following experiments. Predictably, the fluorescence peak of this sensing system undergoes the red-shift and fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with the increase of H2O2 content (Fig. 3D). Meaningly, a good linear relationship between (FL/S) and ln[H2O2] in the scope of 0.2–50 μΜ is observed, supplying a fitting equation of (FL/S) = −0.269 ln[H2O2] + 1.959 with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μM (Fig. 3E). Conversely, the absorption value of 652 nm gradually raises as the H2O2 content increases from 0.2 to 150 μΜ (Fig. 3F). The linear fitting curve of colorimetric assay was noted as A652 = 0.144 ln[H2O2] + 0.343 (R2 = 0.996) within the concentration range of 0.2–50 μΜ, and LOD is determined as 0.13 μM (Fig. 3G). Compared with previously reported methods for H2O2 monitoring, our proposed dual modal strategy demonstrates comparability in terms of analysis performance (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric dual mode sensor for glucose determination
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the upstream molecules can be decomposed to generate H2O2 under the catalysis of corresponding oxidase, which can provide a feasible approach to indirectly detect associated upstream molecules. Especially, glucose can be decomposed into H2O2 and gluconic acid under the catalysis of GOx, and then GL-CDs(Fe) can catalyze oxidation of TMB to form TMBox via produced H2O2. In this regard, ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetric glucose assay based on GL-CDs(Fe) and TMB was proposed (Fig. 4 A). To eliminate the interference of single glucose or GOx on GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system, relevant experiments were carried out. Fig. S13 and Fig. S14 display that either sole glucose or GOx hardly affects the fluorescence profile of GL-CDs(Fe) + TMB system, and yields no absorbance readout at 652 nm. Moreover, the GOx dosage and incubation time are optimized to 0.5 mg mL−1 and 25 min separately for better detection effects (Fig. S15). As expected, the slow decrease of fluorescence intensity and slight red-shift of peak position be observed with the glucose levels growing from 0.8 to 200 μΜ (Fig. 4B). A good linear relationship between (FL/S) and ln[GLU] is established within the range of 0.8–100 μΜ, offering an equation of (FL/S) = −0.296 ln[GLU] + 2.294 (R2 = 0.993) with the LOD of 0.28 μΜ (Fig. 4C). Simultaneously, the UV–vis peak at 652 nm enhances as the increase of glucose concentration, and the color of reaction solution gradually converts from colorless to blue (Fig. 4D). The standard curve plotted in linear range of 0.8–100 μΜ is expressed as A652 = 0.127 ln[GLU] + 0.146 (R2 = 0.993), and the LOD is estimated to be 0.37 μΜ (Fig. 4E). These outcomes demonstrate the excellent sensitivity and wide detection range of our fabricated ratio fluorescence/colorimetric dual mode biosensor for glucose compared with reported sensing platforms (Table S3). Moreover, the ideal selectivity of proposed dual-mode biosensor for glucose analysis is verified by selecting common interfering substances (Fig. 4F and G).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glucose via smartphone-based visual sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To rapidly and conveniently determine glucose, a smartphone-based visual sensing platform has been designed, and specific test process is vividly presented in Fig. 5 A. As the glucose concentration increases from 0.8 to 100 μΜ, the significant color variations of colorless-to-blue occur in reaction solution, and the corresponding photographs are captured by smartphone equipped with color recognition software (Color Grab), which can convert photo information into color parameters according to RGB mode. Obviously, there is an ideal linear relationship between red channel value (R) and ln[GLU], and corresponding linear equation is calculated as R = −16.17 ln[GLU] + 156.73 (R2 = 0.992), and LOD is determined as 0.52 μΜ (Fig. 5B). To evaluate the practicality and reliability of designed biosensor, several human serum specimens are diluted 20-fold, and then the blood glucose content was detected by fluorometric, colorimetric, and smartphone triple modes. As displayed in Table S4, these test outcomes are basically agreement with clinical data with relative error of less than ±8 %, manifesting good reliability and precision of our developed triple mode sensing platform in the probing clinical glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of anti-counterfeiting ink and anticancer cell effect in vitro based on GL-CDs(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the excellent luminescent properties, high water-solubility, and easy availability of GL-CDs(Fe), a simple anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink has been prepared by uniformly dispersing GL-CDs(Fe) solution. Specifically, fluorescent ink is dipped with cotton swab or fine writing brush, and then coated with various patterns on filter paper. After rapid drying, these blue fluorescent patterns including English letter and cartoon picture painted on the filter paper are clearly visible under 365 nm irradiation (Fig. 6 A). This exhibits that the inherent merits of GL-CDs(Fe) are expected to be applied in high-level anti-counterfeiting aspect. In addition to optical properties, the GL-CDs(Fe) with excellent peroxidase-mimetic activity also play an enormous role in anticancer cells. Generally, massive hydroxyl radicals (·OH) with super strong oxidation ability are generated through Fenton-based catalytic effect of GL-CDs(Fe), which can cause the oxidative stress response of tumor cells and further trigger tumor cell apoptosis. To explore the killing ability of GL-CDs(Fe) on cancer cells, the live−dead assays of HepG2 cells stained by Calcein-AM/PI were implemented. Compared with untreated HepG2 cells, the GL-CDs(Fe)-treated HepG2 cells show a remarkably reduced green fluorescence owing to the staining of live cells by Calcein-AM (Fig. 6B). This implies that GL-CDs(Fe) possesses strong ability to convert endogenous H2O2 into ·OH, and can act as an effective chemodynamic reagent for inducing cancer cell apoptosis. Besides, more ·OH are generated through the catalysis of GL-CDs(Fe) with the introduction of H2O2, resulting in the more significant red fluorescence response due to the staining of dead cells by PI (Fig. 6B). These outcomes manifest the enormous potential value of GL-CDs(Fe) in term of anticancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In short, the GL-CDs(Fe) has been synthetized via classic hydrothermal approach exploiting Ganoderma lucidum waste as the precursor. Thanks to their excellent optical property and peroxidase like activity, a novel GL-CDs(Fe)-based fluorescence/colorimetric/smartphone triple mode sensing platform is cleverly fabricated and exploited for accurate detection of glucose in human serum specimens. Furthermore, the GL-CDs(Fe) also presents potential practical application value in fluorescence anti-counterfeiting and cancer cell elimination. Therefore, this efficient “Three-in-One” multifunctional platform based on GL-CDs(Fe) possesses enormous research prospects for bio-sensing, advanced anti-counterfeiting, cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Linjie Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shujun Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yan Liu: Validation, Formal analysis. Yang Ji: Validation, Investigation. Xiaoya Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Fei Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Caolong Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Science and Technology Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301YD0018C) and Key Research and Development Program of Tibet Autonomous Region (XZ202301ZY0015G). The China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2023M733894), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20231021) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2632023GR26). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS, XRD, TEM, and so on.
